items.question: In the bias–variance framework, variance measures instability of predictions across which randomness?
options: Different test inputs x drawn from the feature distribution
Different training datasets D drawn from the same population
Label noise epsilon in a single fixed training set
Different random seeds only during inference
items.answer: Different training datasets D drawn from the same population
items.explanation: Var(ŷ) averages over retraining on new samples D from the same data-generating process. Randomness in x at test time affects prediction error but is not what defines training-set variance.

items.question: Which pattern best signals high variance (overfitting)?
options: Train MSE high, validation MSE equally high
items.explanation: A large train–validation gap with strong training performance indicates the model fit training noise—classic high variance. Dual poor scores suggest high bias instead.

items.question: Which change most directly targets high variance without changing the model class?
options: Use a linear model instead of a tree
Train for more epochs without early stopping on a deep net
items.explanation: More data stabilizes estimates and reduces Var(ŷ). Removing regularization or training longer without stopping usually increases variance; switching model class changes both bias and variance.

items.question: Random forests often beat a single deep decision tree primarily because they reduce:
items.explanation: Individual trees are high-variance; averaging many decorrelated trees lowers Var(ŷ) while keeping expressive power. Bias may change slightly but variance reduction is the main win.
